coredata.dc:description: Lanthanide ion (Ln3+)-doped nanoscale hydroxyapatites (nHAp) with tunable luminescence have attracted increasing attention due to their potential applications as useful biomedical tools (e.g., imaging and clinical therapy). In this study, we reported that doping Terbium (III) ions (Tb3+) in self-activated luminescent nHAp via a facile hydrothermal reaction, using trisodium citrate (Cit3−), generates unique emission-tunable probes known as Cit/Tb-nHAp. The morphology, crystal phase, and luminescence properties of these Cit/Tb-nHAp probes are studied in detail. Moreover, the results demonstrate that the luminescence of self-activated nHAp originates from the carbon dots trapped within the nHAp crystals, in which partial energy transfer occurs from carbon dots (CDs) to Tb3+. The color tunability is successfully achieved by regulating the addition of Cit3−. Biocompatibility study indicates that when co-cultured with C6 glioma cells in vitro for 3 days, ≤800 ppm Cit/Tb-nHAp is not cytotoxic for C6 glioma cells. We also present in vitro data showing efficient cytoplasmic localization of transferrin conjugated Cit/Tb-nHAp into C6 glioma cells by fluorescence cell imaging. We have successfully engineered Cit/Tb-nHAp, a promising biocompatible agent for future in vitro and in vivo fluorescence bioimaging.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectroscopic and electron microscopic analyses of nHAp, Cit-nHAp, and Cit/Tb-nHAp. (a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra, (c – e) TEM, (f – h) SEM, and (i – k) HRTEM images of nHAp (c, f, i), Cit-nHAp (d, g, j), and Cit/Tb-nHAp (e, h, k). Scale bars in TEM and SEM images are 100 nm; CDs are marked as white circles in the HRTEM image of Cit-nHAp, and the inserts in (i-k) are SAED pattern (upper right) and magnified image (bottom right) of each sample.

Confirmation of the luminescence center in Cit-nHAp. Excitation-emission matrix for (a) supernatant of Cit-nHAp and (b) Cit-nHAp (the insets in (a) and (b) are the supernatant and powder under 365 nm UV light, respectively). (c) HRTEM image of particles in the supernatant of Cit-nHAP. UCPL excitation-emission matrix for (d) supernatant of Cit-nHAp, (e) Cit-nHAp. (f) Fluorescence lifetime decay of the Cit-nHAp supernatant and Cit-nHAp.

Luminescent properties of Tb-nHAp, Cit-nHAp, and Cit/Tb-nHAp. PL excitation and emission spectra of (a) Tb-nHAp, (b) Cit-nHAp, (c) PL excitation and emission spectra of Cit1.0, Tb5.0, Tb10.0, Cit1.0Tb5.0, and Cit1.0Tb10.0 (λex = 334 nm). (d) Energy level diagrams of CDs and Tb3+and the energy transfer principle in Cit/Tb-nHAp.

Luminescent color-tunability of Cit/Tb-nHAp probes (Cit0.2Tb5.0, Cit0.5Tb5.0, Cit1.0Tb5.0, and Cit2.0Tb5.0). (a) PL emission spectra (λex = 334 nm). (b) Calculated CIE coordinates from the PL spectra. (c) Digital photographs of powders under indoor room light, 254 nm and 365 nm UV light. (d) Digital photographs of the designed pattern filled with Cit0.2Tb5.0 (left) and Cit2.0Tb5.0 (right) under room light and 254 nm UV light. (e) Fluorescence microscope images of Cit1.0Tb5.0 detected in bright field or using DAPI (blue), FITC (green), and Texas-Red (red) filters. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Cell viability test using the C6 glioma cell line treated with 100 – 800 ppm Cit/Tb-nHAp for 1 day and 3 days. (b) Normalized fluorescence intensities of Cit1.0Tb5.0 in DI water and SBF solution (1 mg/mL) at 37 °C for 0–72 h. (c) Overview of the Tf, Cit/Tb-nHAp conjugation, and cell imaging workflows using Cit/Tb-nHAp and Tf-Cit/Tb-nHAp probes. Fluorescence imaging of C6 glioma cells treated with 200 ppm of (d) Cit/Tb-nHAp and (e) Tf-Cit/Tb-nHAp (scale bars are 20 μm).
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of Cit/Tb-nHAp probes synthesis with Tb3+ doping using Cit3− in a hydrothermal reaction.

head.ce:title: Emission-tunable probes using terbium(III)-doped self-activated luminescent hydroxyapatite for in vitro bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lanthanide ion (Ln3+)-doped nanoscale hydroxyapatites (nHAp) with tunable luminescence have attracted increasing attention due to their potential applications as useful biomedical tools (e.g., imaging and clinical therapy). In this study, we reported that doping Terbium (III) ions (Tb3+) in self-activated luminescent nHAp via a facile hydrothermal reaction, using trisodium citrate (Cit3−), generates unique emission-tunable probes known as Cit/Tb-nHAp. The morphology, crystal phase, and luminescence properties of these Cit/Tb-nHAp probes are studied in detail. Moreover, the results demonstrate that the luminescence of self-activated nHAp originates from the carbon dots trapped within the nHAp crystals, in which partial energy transfer occurs from carbon dots (CDs) to Tb3+. The color tunability is successfully achieved by regulating the addition of Cit3−. Biocompatibility study indicates that when co-cultured with C6 glioma cells in vitro for 3 days, ≤800 ppm Cit/Tb-nHAp is not cytotoxic for C6 glioma cells. We also present in vitro data showing efficient cytoplasmic localization of transferrin conjugated Cit/Tb-nHAp into C6 glioma cells by fluorescence cell imaging. We have successfully engineered Cit/Tb-nHAp, a promising biocompatible agent for future in vitro and in vivo fluorescence bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydroxyapatite (HAp; Ca10(OH)2(PO4)6) has been intensively studied over the past few decades in bone tissue engineering [1–3], drug and gene delivery [4,5], and protein separation [6] owing to its excellent biocompatibility, biodegradable properties, osteoconductivity, non-immunogenicity, and bioactivity. Functionalized nanoscale HAp (nHAp) with novel fluorescent properties, has been uniquely utilized as a bio-probe for practical bio-sensing as well as for cell and tissue imaging [7]. Some studies have focused on the conjugation of fluorescent nHAp with semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), such as ZnS, CdS, CdTe, and CdSe [8–11] or doped with fluorescent organic dyes [12,13]. However, combining fluorescent nHAp materials with semiconductor QDs or organic molecules results in undesirable features for biomedical applications, such as poor biocompatibility, low photostability, photobleaching, and fluorescence quenching. As a doping host, nHAp has been widely used for lanthanide ions (Ln3+)-doping (e.g., Ce3+, Eu3+, Tb3+, and Gd3+) to produce fluorescence because Ln3+ ions have similar radii to the Ca2+ ions in the HAp crystal structure [14–16]. Since Ln3+-doped nHAp can overcome the low fluorescence intensity and poor light stability of traditional fluorescent dyes [17], while also avoiding toxicity and disposal issues related to semiconductor QDs [18], they could be suitable for use in bioimaging of mammalian cells [19,20].

In parallel, self-activated luminescent nHAp which emits blue light, has also attracted a great deal of interest. Due to its unique, environmentally-friendly, biocompatible, and photostable properties, self-activated luminescent nHAp has been used for drug delivery [21], bioimaging [22], and tissue engineering [23]. Previous studies have shown that such self-activated blue emission may be derived from the CO2
− radical impurities in the nHAp crystal lattice which lacks rare earth or transition metal ions as activators [24]. However, the application and fluorescence properties improvement of self-activated luminescent nHAp is limited due to the ambiguity of the mechanism of fluorescence. Recent data suggest that trisodium citrate (Cit3−) and the pH used during synthesis may be significant factors that influence the fluorescence properties of nHAp [25]. The clues mentioned above may be promising in revealing further insights into the mechanism behind “self-activated” luminescence.

Doping Ln3+ into a host structure is a proper approach to develop fluorescent probes because the emission spectra of doping ions include strong and broad bands of 4f – 4f or 5d – 4f transitions [26]. Energy transfer from a light sensitizer to an energy acceptor is essential for the modulation of fluorescent materials, both in theory and in practice [27]. More specifically, the use of Ln3+-doped multi-emission probes excited with a single wavelength is an emerging technology due to their extensive biomedical applications (e.g., in imaging and clinical therapy) [28–30]. With the increasing need for multicolor emission probes, color tuning of single-phase luminescent nanomaterials via the energy transfer among Ln3+ ions with various doping procedures has recently garnered significant attention [31,32]. Studies have shown that Tb3+ can be employed as an activator in different hosts using an excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 380 nm and its emission spectrum is based on 5D3–7FJ (blue) and 5D4–7FJ (green) transitions [33]. The energy transfer processes of Terbium(III) ions (Tb3+) and Europium(III) ions (Eu3+) in co-doping hosts have been intensively investigated to obtain the correct color tuning [7,27,34]. Notably, no systematic study has reported on the energy transfer between “self-activated” nHAp and Ln3+, which is expected to induce a tunable multicolor emission.

Herein, we report a facile synthesis of Tb3+-doped self-activated luminescent nHAp probes via hydrothermal synthesis using Cit3− (Cit/Tb-nHAp). In this study, we also investigated the effect of Cit3− and Tb3+ on the crystal phases, morphologies, and luminescent properties of nHAp. Possible mechanisms for self-activated luminescent nHAp (Cit-nHAp) and energy transfer in Cit/Tb-nHAp probes are also systematically discussed. By merely regulating the amount of Cit3− per reaction, the emission of the probes can be tuned from blue to green. Moreover, the photostability of the Cit/Tb-nHAp probes in simulated body fluid (SBF) solution and in deionized (DI) water was analyzed. Furthermore, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of the Cit/Tb-nHAp probe using C6 glioma cells. Additionally, the cell imaging applications of the Cit/Tb-nHAp probes were also examined following conjugation with transferrin (Tf) using C6 glioma cells. Hence, Cit/Tb-nHAp probes with tunable emission may be promising agents for cell and tissue imaging and/or targeted biological therapies.

Ammonium phosphate dibasic ((NH4)2HPO4, ≥98%), Trisodium citrate dihydrate (Cit3− hydrate, ≥99%), ammonia solution (NH3‧H2O, 28–30%), Calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2‧4H2O, 99%), terbium(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Tb(NO3)3‧5H2O, 99.9%), and citric acid (≥99.5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (MO, USA). Ethyl alcohol (95%) was ordered from SK Chemicals Inc. (Suwon, Korea), and SBF was purchased from Biosesang Inc. (Seongnam, Korea).

In a typical experiment, 1.4 mmol (NH4)2HPO4 and 0.2 mmol Cit3− hydrate were dissolved into 30 mL DI water to form solution A, which was adjusted to pH 10 with NH3‧H2O (28%). Next, 3.5 mmol Ca(NO3)2‧4H2O and a stoichiometric amount of Tb(NO3)3‧5H2O were added to 20 mL of DI water to make solution B. Then solution B was mixed into solution A and stirred for 30 min. The suspension was transferred to a Teflon®-lined stainless steel autoclave that was maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling the reactor to room temperature (25 °C), the precipitate was purified by centrifugation, washed with DI water and ethyl alcohol three times, and finally dried into a powder under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. The molar ratios of Tb/(Ca + Tb) were initially set at 0.025, 0.050, 0.075, 0.100, and 0.125 and the following nomenclature was used to depict this: Cit0.2Tb2.5, Cit0.2Tb5.0, Cit0.2Tb7.5, Cit0.2Tb10.0, and Cit0.2Tb12.5, respectively. In subsequent experiments, the molar ratios of Tb/(Ca + Tb) were fixed at 0.050 while different amount of Cit3− were used (i.e., 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 mmol). The following nomenclature was used to depict this: Cit0.2Tb5.0, Cit0.5Tb5.0, Cit1.0Tb5.0, Cit2.0Tb5.0, respectively. In addition, Cit1.0, Tb5.0, Tb10.0, and Cit1.0Tb10.0 were prepared as control samples. All experimental details are listed in Table S1.

The crystal phases of the samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Empyrean series-2; Netherlands) of CNU chemistry Core Facility using Cu-Kα radiation. The quantitative analyses of samples were conducted using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Avio500, Perkin-Elmer, USA) after the samples were completely dissolved in 0.1 M HCl. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using an FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 2, PerkinElmer, UK). The morphology and size of the samples were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-7600, Hitachi, Japan). Nanostructure and crystallographic analysis were performed using high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Tecnai F30 S-Twin, FEI, Netherlands). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of the samples was measured using a lifetime spectrophotometer (TM-200 LED strobe, 3113, PTI, USA) at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained with X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (MultiLab 2000, Thermo Scientific, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, S-1000, Scinco, Korea) was carried out by heating the samples at a rate of 20 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. The fluorescence of the Cit1.0Tb5.0 powder was observed with a fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse 80i, Tokyo, Japan) using DAPI, FITC, and Texas-Red filters. The mean hydrodynamic diameter and the zeta potential of the samples were measured using a Zetasizer (ZS Nano, Malvern Instruments, UK).

To test the photostability of Cit/Tb-nHAp, Cit1.0Tb5.0 was dispersed into either DI water or SBF solution at a final concentration of 1 mg/mL−1. These experiments were carried out in triplicate. The fluorescence intensities of each solution were successively monitored after 0, 0.5, 1, 4, 24, and 72 h at 37 °C to confirm its compatibility and fluorescence performance in the SBF solution before further in vitro experiments.

The C6 rat glioma cell line was purchased from the Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, Korea). C6 cells were incubated with Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM; 11995–065, Gibco, CA, USA), 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS; 16000, Gibco, CA, USA), and 1% penicillin–streptomycin (15140122, Gibco, CA, USA) at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Every 2–3 days, cells were subcultured using 0.25% trypsin (25200056, Gibco, CA, USA) digestion. Cells were directly frozen down to −80 °C using a cell banker (11889, Zenoaq, Fukushima, Japan) and stored at −80 °C until further use.

A thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT; 0793, Amresco, OH, USA) assay was performed to determine Cit/Tb-nHAp cytotoxicity. C6 cells were plated at a density of 2 × 103 cells/well in a 96-well culture plate (SPL Life Science, Pocheon, Korea). One day after seeding, the medium was supplemented with 100 ppm, 200 ppm, 400 ppm, and 800 ppm Cit/Tb-nHAp, and cells were incubated for 1 day and 3 days. After the appropriate incubation time, the medium was replaced with 100 µL of fresh medium containing MTT (10 μL, 5 mg/mL), and cultures were left to incubate for 4 h. Next, 100 µL of dimethyl sulfoxide (D1022, Biosesang, Seongnam, Korea) was added to dissolve the intracellular blue formazan crystals, and absorbance at 570 nm was measured using a Multiskan EX microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA). Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

Cit1.0Tb5.0 was chosen as the cell imaging agent because it shows strong fluorescence and homogeneous morphology. A further stabilization step was followed before the biological experiment. Briefly, 30 mg of Cit1.0Tb5.0 was dispersed in 3 mL of citric acid solution (0.6 mg/mL−1). The solution was then sonicated using an ultrasonic probe (pulse mode, 5 s on/off) with 20% amplitude (VCX750, Sonics & Materials, CT, USA) for 2 h, followed by washing with DI water thrice, and storage at 4 °C. Tf-conjugated Cit/Tb-nHAp (Cit1.0Tb5.0) probes were then prepared for cell imaging. Briefly, each aqueous solution of 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC; 2 mg/50 μL, TCI America, OR, USA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 2 mg/50 μL, Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), and Tf (200 μg/30 μL, Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) were mixed into 100 mL of Cit/Tb-nHAp colloidal solution and stirred at 25 °C for 2 h. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min and used for further cell imaging experiments. C6 glioma cells were seeded into a 24-well plate (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) containing 0.4% gelatin-coated cover glass. After incubation for 24 h, cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA; P2031, Biosesang, Seongnam, Korea) for 30 min and rinsed with Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS; 14190144, Gibco, CA, USA). Next, the fixed C6 cells were incubated with the Cit/Tb-nHAp probes (200 ppm) overnight at 4 °C. C6 cells were then washed with DPBS to remove the excess probe. Finally, the samples were imaged under a confocal microscope (LSM700, Carl Zeiss, Jena, Germany) equipped with a digital camera. Imaging of Cit/Tb-nHAp was performed using an argon laser (λex = 488 nm), and emission was detected as a green color between 450 nm and 520 nm.

The facile hydrothermal synthesis of Cit/Tb-nHAp probes is depicted in Scheme 1 . After solution B was introduced into solution A, amorphous calcium phosphate nuclei (Fig. S1) were first formed through the reaction between Ca2+ and PO4 3− (OH−). The simultaneous incorporation of Tb3+ with amorphous calcium phosphate nuclei takes place either by substitution of Ca2+ ions or dopant insertion in vacancy sites [33]. At the same time, sufficient hydrothermal treatment induces the pyrolysis of Cit3− to form one kind of luminescence centers that emit blue fluorescent light. Subsequently, Tb3+ and a portion of luminescence centers can be gradually doped into the HAp lattice during the growth of the HAp crystals.

To investigate the influence of Cit3− and Tb3+ on the HAp nanocrystal phase, nHAp, Cit-nHAp, and Cit/Tb-nHAp were chosen to compare the physical properties, morphology, and crystal structure. For comparison, nHAp was obtained using a surfactant-free hydrothermal method aided by co-precipitation. Cit-nHAp was synthesized with 1.0 mmol Cit3− (Cit1.0), and Cit/Tb-nHAp is the representative sample, Cit1.0Tb5.0. The XRD patterns of nHAp, Cit-nHAp, and Cit/Tb-nHAp (Fig. 1 a) show that their diffraction peaks are indexed to the HAp hexagonal phase (ICDD No.09-0432). The cell parameters and crystallinity of samples are displayed in Table S2, the lattice parameters for both ɑ and c decreased in Cit-nHAp, and the doping of Tb3+ apparently induced the increase of both ɑ and c. Moreover, compared to nHAp, the crystallinities of Cit-nHAp and Cit/Tb-nHAp were evidently decreased. And the quantitative analysis of the prepared samples using ICP-OES (Table S3) shows that the Ca/P ratios of three samples match the theoretical ratio, and Tb/Ca ratio in Cit/Tb-nHAp is 0.060, which is also close to the designed ratio. Analyses of FTIR (Fig. 1b) show that the peaks at 3570 cm−1 and 629–630 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching modes and librational vibrations of the OH− group in HAp. The typical PO4 3− bands can be observed at 1090–1092 cm−1 and 1024–1028 cm−1 (ν 3), 961–962 cm−1 (ν 1), 600–602 cm−1 and 563 cm−1 (ν 4), and at 470–474 cm−1 (ν 2). Additionally, the peak at 871–875 cm−1 is ascribed to ν 2 CO3 2− bands (bending modes), which induced by B-type substitution (CO3 2− replacing PO4 3−). The strong band at 1573–1579 cm−1 and 1413–1414 cm−1 for Cit-nHAp and Cit/Tb-nHAp were attributed to COO− asymmetric stretching and COO− symmetric stretching absorption, respectively, owing to the adsorption of Cit3− on the surface of the HAp particles. To investigate the effect of Cit3− and Tb3+ on the morphology of our samples, SEM and TEM characterizations were performed. Without Cit3−, the nHAp crystals displayed a short nanorod (NRs) shape with a length of 51.7 ± 18.8 nm and a diameter of 26.6 ± 6.0 nm (Fig. 1c). These NRs changed their morphology and became drastically thinner (8.7 ± 2.0 nm) while still maintaining a similar length (51.1 ± 9.7 nm) following the addition of Cit3− into the reaction (Fig. 1d). Moreover, Tb3+ ion-induced thin NRs assembly resulted in thicker rod-shaped nanocrystallites (Fig. 1e). SEM images (Fig. 1f – h) further confirm the morphological changes of samples following the addition of Cit3− and Tb3+ in the synthesis reactions. The morphological changes generated in HAp NRs using different Tb3+ doping ratios were also studied (Fig. S5). From Cit0.2Tb2.5 to Cit0.2Tb7.5, no obvious morphological changes were observed, while NRs became irregular and needle-like with increasing Tb3+ ratios up to 0.100 (Cit0.2Tb10.0) and 0.125 (Cit0.2Tb12.5). Excessive Tb3+ doping may influence the nucleation rate and growth direction of the lattice plane, resulting in HAp lattice distortion [35]. Cit3− also affects the morphology of HAp nanocrystals (Fig. S5), where no distinct changes in morphology and size were observed from Cit0.2Tb5.0 up to Cit1.0Tb5.0. However, the morphology of Cit2.0Tb5.0 shows distinct staggered needle-like nanocrystallites. Cit3− was most likely adsorbed onto the surface of HAp due to the good match between the spacing of the terminal COO– group in Cit3− and the lattice parameter c in apatite [36]. What's more, the adsorption density of Cit3− increased with the addition of Cit3−. Cit3− may selectively absorb on the crystal facets along the anisotropic growth direction, resulting in an increase in the aspect ratio of HAp NRs and formation of the needle-like structures [37].

HRTEM was also carried out (Fig. 1i – k) to characterize differences in the nanocrystal structure among nHAp, Cit-nHAp, and Cit/Tb-nHAp. The three samples possess the typical identical lattice spacing at the (002) hexagonal HAp crystal except for the d-spacing of 0.279 nm, which corresponds to the (211) planes in nHAp. The selected area electron diffraction patterns (SAED; upper right insets in Fig. 1i – k) of the three samples provide evidence to support the growth direction. Aside from morphological changes, the addition of Cit3− also induced a dramatic decrease in the crystallinity of nHAp. Moreover, the Cit/Tb-nHAp HRTEM images clearly show that NRs composed of thinner 20 nm long nanorod crystallites with preferential growth along the longitudinal direction (c-axis) of the NRs, which suggests that assembly of primary particles through oriented attachment is involved in the formation of these NRs [38]. These crystallization and growth processes are universally observed in bio-mineralization inspired crystallization mechanisms [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of luminescence center of Cit-nHAp
ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, a series of self-activated fluorescent nHAp materials have attracted considerable interest. Most of them are thought to be radical defect-related luminescence, and the mechanism behind their luminescence is ambiguous and usually overlooked [23,40]. Previous studies have reported that the fluorescence intensity of “self-activated” fluorescent nHAp relies on the amount of Cit3− added and the pH of the reaction solution. However, other studies have shown that sodium citrate and citric acid have been used as carbon sources for the synthesis of blue fluorescent CDs [41,42]. Interestingly, recent literature has also revealed that in fluorescent silicon nanoparticles, CDs formed through the reaction of citrate or citric acid with amine-compounds ((3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane) are responsible for the optical properties of the products and not the silicon itself [43]. Thus, we hypothesize that self-activation may be achieved because the CDs are the luminescence center. To explore the mechanism behind the self-activation of fluorescent nHAp, both supernatant and precipitate (Cit-nHAp) in the final reaction solution were studied and characterized. The maximum emission peak of the Cit-nHAP supernatant at 435 nm is independent of the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2 a). Additionally, the maximum emission of this fraction is observed under excitation at 380 nm and 300 nm. The supernatant solution also exhibited strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) light (inset in Fig. 2a). The excitation-emission matrix of Cit-nHAp shows an excitation-dependent emission phenomenon with a redshift from 428 nm to 439 nm when excited from 334 nm to 378 nm (Fig. 2b). Like the supernatant solution, Cit-nHAp powder emits vivid blue light upon excitation with UV light at 365 nm (inset in Fig. 2b). HRTEM imaging of the supernatant (Fig. 2c) shows small spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of 4.90 ± 0.99 nm, which were well dispersed without any visible aggregation. The magnified HRTEM imaging of nanoparticles (inset in Fig. 2c) shows well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.212 nm, which is close to the (110) diffraction facet of graphite carbon [43–45]. The up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) was also detected in the supernatant solution (Fig. 2d). The supernatant solution emits UCPL with the maximum emission at 435 nm following excitation at 755 nm. UCPL, a common phenomenon observed in fluorescent carbon materials, ascribed to both the surface state and quantum size effect [46,47]. Similar to the supernatant, Cit-nHAP precipitate also displays UCPL properties (Fig. 2e). However, the maximum emission locates at 428 nm when the excitation wavelength is set at 669 nm. The τ of the supernatant and Cit-nHAp were measured as 6.2 and 4.8 ns, respectively (Fig. 2f). The similarity between the τ of the supernatant and Cit-nHAp samples confirms that the blue luminescence of Cit-nHAp is due to the entrapment of CDs in the HAp host lattice or interstitial spaces of the crystal structure. Furthermore, some areas with lattices going in a different direction were observed in the Cit-nHAp crystal structure (Fig. 1j). The size of these areas corresponds with the above CDs in the supernatant, thereby suggesting that CDs were indeed trapped in the interstitial sites of the HAp lattice via a heterogeneous nucleation and growth process, and those results accord with what Jang et al. firstly reported [48]. After being trapped in the HAp nanoparticles, the surface state of the CDs may undergo some change. Therefore, there may be little difference in the excitation-emission matrix and τ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy transfer principle and luminescent color-tunability
ce:section.ce:para: Ln3+ doped fluorescent nHAps have been reported to be useful in high-performance optical applications [49,50]. The ions radii and coordination environments of Tb3+ and Ca2+ are similar. Therefore, Tb3+ could be doped into HAp with the replacement of Ca2+ or insertion into Ca2+ vacancy sites [33]. The PL excitation and emission spectra of Tb-nHAP (Fig. 3 a) show narrow excitation bands ranging from 300 to 500 nm with a peak at 351 nm and four emission peaks (488 nm, 544 nm, 584 nm, and 622 nm) corresponding to the 5D4 → 7Fj (j = 6, 5, 4, 3) transitions of Tb3+. The strongest emission peak occurs at 544 nm, indicating that the samples emit green light. The excitation and emission spectra of Cit-nHAp display the strongest emission peak at 428 nm following excitation at 334 nm, which is consistent with the excitation-emission matrix data (Fig. 3b). The emission spectra of Cit-nHAp (350–550 nm) partly overlaps with the excitation spectra of Tb-nHAp (300 – 500 nm), suggesting the possibility of energy transfer from CDs to Tb3+. To deeply investigate the energy transfer strategy, PL spectra of Cit1.0, Tb5.0, Tb10.0, Cit1.0Tb5.0, and Cit1.0Tb10.0 which are excited at 334 nm are compared (Fig. 3c). For Tb5.0 and Tb10.0, only the typical weak Tb emission peaks (488 nm, 544 nm, 584 nm, and 622 nm) were observed, and Tb10.0 displayed a higher signal intensity than Tb5.0. However, when Cit3− was added at the same time as Tb3+ doping, the intensities of the Tb3+ emission peaks increased significantly. By comparing PL spectra among Cit1.0.0, Cit1.0Tb5.0, and Cit1.0Tb10.0, we observed that the intensities of the Tb3+ PL spectra peaks increased with the increase of Tb ratio. In contrast, the CDs peak intensities gradually decreased. These data suggest that co-doping of Tb3+ ions and CDs weakens CDs emission and activates Tb3+ ions emission, further highlighting the possibility of energy transfer from CDs to Tb3+. Additionally, the Cit1.0Tb5.0 PL excitation spectrum (Fig. S6) includes all spectral features for both Tb3+ (317 nm, 368 nm, and 377 nm) and CDs (284 nm, 300–350 nm). The mechanism of partial energy transfer from CDs to Tb3+ is illustrated in Fig. 3d. Generally, Cit-nHAp emits blue light while Tb3+ ions emit green light. After co-doping CDs and Tb3+, partial energy transfer from CDs to Tb3+ occurs in Cit/Tb-nHAp. Following interactions with 334 nm photons, CDs electrons which are generated by the absorption of short-wavelength light (i.e., highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO)) can relax to the HOMO state through radiative recombination. This change leads to partial energy transfer to Tb3+ ions, inducing the excited state of Tb3+ ions. Excited electrons in Tb3+ return to the lowest vibrational level of the excited state (5D4) through vibration relaxation. These excited electrons finally return to the ground state by emitting light of different wavelengths (5D4 to 7Fj, j = 6, 5, 4, 3).

We also studied the effect of the Tb3+ activator ion ratio on the Tb3+ characteristic luminescence intensity of Cit/Tb-nHAp (Cit3− addition is 0.2 mmol). The PL emission spectra (Fig. S7) show that the four characteristic peaks (488 nm, 544 nm, 584 nm, and 622 nm) of each sample remain present, and the PL intensity of the 5D4 to 7F4 transition reaches a maximum at a Tb3+ doping ratio of 0.075. However, the intensity begins to decrease when the doping ratio of Tb3+ increases, which is owing to the lack of sufficient luminescent centers in a phenomenon known as concentration quenching. The concentration quenching effect of Ln3+ activated materials was mostly caused by the non-radiative energy transfer from Ln3+ ions to nearby quenching sites, most likely by exchange and multipole-multipole interactions. Besides, other nonradioactive processes such as energy transfer to the hydroxyl ions or defects in HAp could also induce luminescence quenching [34,51].

To confirm the composition of Tb3+ and CDs codoped-nHAp, the XPS of Cit1.0Tb5.0 was conducted. The peaks P 2p, P 2s, C 1s, Ca 2p, Ca 2s, O 1s, Tb 3d5/2, and Tb 3d3/2 in the full XPS spectrum (Fig. S8a) was observed. After peak fitting, four peaks appeared in the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S8b) which were attributed to CC/CH (284.8 eV), CN (286.0 eV), CO/CN (287.9 eV), and CO3 2−/–COOH (289.1 eV), respectively. A weak N 1 s peak at approximately 400 eV that originated from N-rich CDs was also observed in Cit-nHAp (Fig. S8c). The Ca 2p spectrum (Fig. S8d) shows the two typical peaks at 347.3 eV and 350.8 eV due to the Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 contributions, respectively. The two fitting peaks of the P 2p spectrum (Fig. S8e) at 133.0 eV and 134.1 eV are individually attributed to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2. A narrow scan of the Tb 3d spectrum (Fig. S8f) shows two peaks at 1241.3 eV (Tb 3d5/2) and 1276.0 eV (Tb 3d3/2), confirming the successful doping of Tb3+ in nHAp structures. The molar concentrations of elements deduced from XPS spectra are presented in Table S4. These XPS data imply that CDs and Tb3+ were successfully codoped in the HAp nanocrystals. TGA data for nHAp, Cit-nHAP, and Tb-nHAP (Fig. S9) suggest that the initial weight loss of 2% in HAp and 5% in Cit-nHAp and Cit/Tb-nHAp before 200 °C was caused by the thermal desorption of physically adsorbed water in the powder. The subsequent thermogravimetric weight loss between 200 and 700 °C was attributed to the decomposition of CDs trapped in nHAp and the pyrolysis of citrate combined on the surface of the nanoparticles.

To fully explore the luminescent color tunability of Cit/Tb-nHAp, a series of Cit/Tb-nHAP samples were tested. First, the Tb3+ doping ratio was fixed at 0.050, while the Cit3− concentration was titrated from 0.2 mmol to 2.0 mmol. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the emission spectrum only consisted of typical Tb3+ peaks when 0.2 mmol Cit3− was used. However, when Cit3− increased to 0.5 mmol, a strong and broad peak of CDs was observed. As the Cit3− concentration increased to 1.0 mmol, the intensities of the three characteristic peaks at 488 nm, 544 nm, and 582 nm also increased, with two of these peaks (544 nm and 584 nm) reaching the highest intensities. However, at 2.0 mmol Cit3−, the peak intensities at 544 nm and 584 nm did not increase any further, unlike the peak at 488 nm. Considering the excitation and emission intensities, as well as the poor morphology of Cit2.0Tb5.0, no further more Cit3− addition experiments were performed. The CIE chromaticity diagram for Cit0.2Tb5, Cit0.5Tb5, Cit1.0Tb5, and Cit2.0 are depicted in Fig. 4b. The CIE coordinates vary systematically from blue to cyan with the decrease of Cit3− addition when excited at 334 nm. Digital photographs of Cit/Tb-nHAp powder under 254 nm and 365 nm UV irradiation show that each of the samples is easily distinguishable from one another by color, especially when excited at 254 nm, which induces vivid color variations from green to cyan and blue (Fig. 4c). We also show that patterns created using Cit/Tb-nHAp are not visible under normal daylight but are instead revealed under a UV lamp (254 nm) where it emits a bright green/blue color (Fig. 4d). Consequently, Cit/Tb-nHAp has the potential to be used as an anti-counterfeiting measure. Besides, using DAPI (blue), FITC (green), and Texas-Red (red) filters under a microscope, Cit1.0Tb5.0 powder appears bright blue, green, and red, respectively (Fig. 4e). The multicolor fluorescence optical feature of Cit/Tb-nHAp make it a potentially useful tool for in vitro and in vivo cell imaging to, for example, track the movement of cells from one tissue to another (e.g., from the gallbladder (green) to blood (red), or vice versa). Furthermore, if a drug is conjugated to a Cit/Tb-nHAp probe, the multicolor fluorescence of these probes may allow drugs to be tracked within different niches of the body, including, but not limited to, the gallbladder and blood. Therefore, Cit/Tb-nHAp probes could be developed into invaluable biological tools to study drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation of probes and in vitro bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: The mean hydrodynamic diameter and the zeta potential measurements were performed on Cit/Tb-nHAp probes before and after critic acid stabilization. Fig. S10a provides straightforward evidence for the well-dispersed state of NRs. The hydrodynamic diameter of Cit/Tb-nHAp (Fig. S10c) sharply decreased and reached 80.7 ± 0.15 nm, which is the length of a single NR. The zeta potential of Cit/Tb-nHAp (Fig. S10c) also increased and reached − 20.9 ± 2.51 mV, suggesting that agglomeration decreased significantly due to strong electrostatic repulsion among the particles. Cit/Tb-nHAp probe cytotoxicity was assessed by culturing C6 glioma cells in the presence of Cit/Tb-nHAp probes at various concentrations (100 – 800 ppm) for 1 day and 3 days (Fig. 5 a). Results from the MTT assay revealed that Cit/Tb-nHAp (up to 800 ppm) is not toxic to C6 glioma cells even after 3 days of culture.

Due to its outstanding biocompatibility and non-toxicity, bioimaging is one of the most important potential applications for fluorescent Cit/Tb-nHAp. Accordingly, to better analyze the optical properties and stability of the as-obtained Cit/Tb-nHAp, we tested the fluorescence intensity of Cit1.0Tb5.0 in DI water and SBF solution (1 mg/mL) at 37 °C. All of the intensities were normalized to the intensity in DI water at 0 h. After treatment for 24–72 h, we found that the fluorescent signal intensities were relatively stable (over 50% of relative intensity; Fig. 5b). Thus, this high biocompatibility and stable fluorescence signal in the SBF solution suggest that Cit/Tb-nHAp probes may be used as effective in vitro and in vivo bioimaging tools.

The Tf receptor is a cell-membrane internalizing receptor that is responsible for almost all of iron sequestration in mammalian cells. Tf is overexpressed in various malignant tumors [13,52]. We used Tf in the cell imaging experiment due to its tumor-targeting capabilities, and because it could be conjugated to Cit/Tb-nHAp using EDC-NHS coupling chemistry [53]. The successful conjugation of Tf on the surface of particles was confirmed by TEM, hydrodynamic diameter, zeta potential, and XPS. TEM image of Tf-Cit//Tb-nHAp (Fig. S10b) shows the blurred boundary of NRs compared to citric acid stabilized NRs (Fig. S10a), which is probably due to the thin Tf layer adsorbed on the surface of NRs. Furthermore, the increase of hydrodynamic diameter and the change of zeta potential from negative to positive (Fig. S10c) also verified the Tf functionalization. XPS analyses were carried out to characterize the chemical composition and surface states of Tf-Cit/Tb-nHAp (Fig. S11). XPS survey scan (Fig. S11a) exhibits the peaks of P 2p, P 2 s, C1s, Ca 2p, N1s, O1s, Tb 3d5/2, and Tb 3d3/2. The C1s XPS (Fig. S11b) can be fitted with four binding energy at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, 288.0 eV, and 289.1 eV, which are assigned to the CC/CH, CN, CO/CN, and CO3 2−/–COOH, respectively and shows the higher ratio of CC/CH and CN. And complete N1s spectrum (Fig. S11c) was also obtained, which clarified the presence of the protein molecule. Additionally, as the existence of disulfide bonds in the Tf molecule, the S 2p spectrum (Fig. S11d) with a peak at 163.7 eV further discloses the functionalization of the Tf layer on the surface of NRs. Table S4 shows the molar concentrations of elements on the surface of Tf-Cit/Tb-nHAp deduced from XPS spectra. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the in vitro cell imaging protocol for C6 glioma cells was used to demonstrate the biomedical application of Cit/Tb-nHAp. Fig. 5d, 5e show the fluorescence imaging of C6 glioma cells labeled with Cit/Tb-nHAp and Tf-Cit/Tb-nHAp fluorescent probes, respectively. Following the treatment of C6 glioma cells with Cit/Tb-nHAp for 24 h, only a weak nonspecific green fluorescent signal was observed (Fig. 5d). In contrast, after cells were labeled with Tf-Cit/Tb-nHAp, the fluorescent signal was localized uniformly in the cytoplasm (Fig. 5e). This Tf-Cit/Tb-nHAp induced fluorescence also seemed to show some specificity for the cytoplasm as the cell nuclei were clearly distinguishable. These data clearly show that Cit/Tb-nHAp can enter cells via endocytosis with the aid of the Tf receptor, resulting in a stable intracellular fluorescent signal. The active surface of Cit/Tb-nHAp probes can be easily modified using tumor-targeting ligands. Therefore, we anticipate the development of Cit/Tb-nHAp into valuable tumor targeting and bioimaging tools.

We have successfully engineered novel fluorescent Cit/Tb-nHAp probes with a tunable emission spectrum (blue to green) via a one-step hydrothermal reaction with the assistance of Cit3−. After analyzed the morphologies and luminescent properties of both supernatant and precipitate fractions of the Cit-nHAp reaction solution, we have confirmed that the luminescent center of Cit-nHAp is CDs trapped in the nHAp crystal structure rather than CO2
− radical impurities in the crystal lattice [21,23]. Furthermore, we demonstrate that there exists a partial transfer of energy from CDs to Tb3+ in the obtained Cit/Tb-nHAp, which was never reported previously. The emission color of the probes can be tuned from blue to green by simple regulation of the amount of Cit3− dopant. Moreover, the Cit/Tb-nHAp probes maintained good photostability in SBF compared to DI water. Significantly, these Cit/Tb-nHAp probes displayed excellent biocompatibility (over 88% cell viability up to 800 ppm of Cit/Tb-nHAp for up to 3 days). Following conjugation with Tf, Cit/Tb-nHAp probes successfully entered C6 glioma cells, inducing fluorescent labeling of the cells. Thus, Cit/Tb-nHAp probes hold significant promise for use in luminescence and bioimaging. And the further application of this fluorescent nHAp will focus on drug delivery, therapy, and in vivo bioimaging.
